rules.rule_statement: Intestacy describes the state or condition of a person who died (i.e., the decedent) without a valid will. Intestate succession is the default statutory distribution scheme that disposes of any part of a decedent’s estate that was not effectively disposed of by a valid will or nonprobate instrument. Intestate succession statutes vary from state to state. But they generally favor the decedent’s surviving spouse and issue, followed by the decedent’s other relations. Intestacy laws direct that property escheat (i.e., revert) to the state only if none of the statutory takers survives the decedent. The actual intent of the decedent is irrelevant to how property passes under the laws of intestacy. Heirs are individuals who are entitled to a decedent’s property under intestate succession. If a will fails to dispose of all of the decedent’s property, use the rules of intestacy to determine how property not mentioned in the will is to be disposed. Cal. Prob. Code § 6400.

Intestacy

Intestate succession is the default statutory distribution scheme that disposes of any part of a decedent’s estate that was not effectively disposed of by a valid will or nonprobate instrument. Intestate succession statutes vary from state to state. But they generally favor the decedent’s surviving spouse and issue, followed by the decedent’s other relations. Intestacy laws direct that property escheat (i.e., revert) to the state only if none of the statutory takers survives the decedent. The actual intent of the decedent is irrelevant to how property passes under the laws of intestacy. Heirs are individuals who are entitled to a decedent’s property under intestate succession. If a will fails to dispose of all of the decedent’s property, use the rules of intestacy to determine how property not mentioned in the will is to be disposed. Cal. Prob. Code § 6400.

rules.rule_statement: In framing an intestacy statute, lawmakers’ primary policy is to carry out the probable intent of the average intestate decedent. The most common statutory scheme assumes that the decedent would wish for her surviving spouse or domestic partner to succeed to all of her property if she had no surviving issue and otherwise to share her property with her surviving issue.

rules.source_text:
Generally
In framing an intestacy statute, lawmakers’ primary policy is to carry out the probable intent of the average intestate decedent. The most common statutory scheme assumes that the decedent would wish for her surviving spouse or domestic partner to succeed to all of her property if she had no surviving issue and otherwise to share her property with her surviving issue.

rules.rule_statement: Community property is property acquired during marriage; it includes all earnings and all property acquired with those earnings. All debts incurred during marriage are community-property debts. Quasi-community property is generally all property acquired by a married couple while living outside California that would have been characterized as community property had the couple been living in California when the property was acquired. Quasi-community property is distributed in the same manner as community property. However, real property located outside California is subject to the intestacy laws of the jurisdiction in which the property is located. When classifying the decedent’s property as separate, community, or quasi?community property, recall that property acquired by gift or inheritance is separate, as is any other property that is traceable to separate property. A will may transmute property (i.e., change its classification) if the will (1) clearly expresses the intention to change the nature of the property and (2) is signed by the adversely affected spouse. But a statement in the will regarding the character of property is not admissible as evidence of transmutation in any proceeding that occurs before the testator’s death.

rules.rule_statement: The Uniform Simultaneous Death Act (USDA) provides that when there is insufficient evidence of the order of two individuals’ deaths, the property of each individual passes as though he had survived the other individual. The USDA requires clear and convincing evidence that an heir or devisee survived the decedent by 120 hours to take under the decedent’s will (unless otherwise specified). Similarly, an heir must survive the decedent by 120 hours to take by intestacy. The 120-hour rule does not apply if its application would result in an escheat to the state. In California, when spouses or domestic partners die simultaneously, one?half of the couple’s community property and quasi-community property is included in the estate of one, and the other half is included in the estate of the other. Rule In California, the 120-hour rule applies only to intestate succession.

rules.rule_statement: The determination of death under either the modern or common-law standard must adhere to usual and customary standards of medical practice. In most instances, death can be determined based on the irreversible cessation of circulatory and respiratory functions. California defines “death” as the irreversible cessation of (1) circulatory and respiratory functions or (2) all functions of the entire brain.

rules.rule_statement: In the case of an insured and her beneficiary, when it cannot be determined whether the insured or the beneficiary died first, the proceeds are treated as if the insured survived the beneficiary. rules.rule_statement: A child of a marriage or domestic partnership is presumed to be the natural child of the parties. A child conceived before but born after the death of his mother’s husband is a posthumously born child. Rule In California, a rebuttable presumption exists that a child is the natural child of the deceased husband if the child is born within 300 days of the husband’s death. A child born to the mother more than 300 days after the husband’s death has a burden to prove that he is the deceased husband’s natural child. California prohibits a parent from inheriting from or through a child based on the parent-child relationship if any of the following applies: ii) The parent did not acknowledge the child; or iii) The parent abandoned the child during the child’s minority for at least seven consecutive years that continued until the end of the child’s minority, with the intent to abandon the child. Failing to provide support or to communicate with the child for the prescribed period is presumptive evidence of the intent to abandon. A parent to whom any of the above factors applies does not inherit through intestacy and is deemed to have predeceased the child.

rules.rule_statement: References in a will to “children” include adopted children. Adoption curtails all inheritance rights between the natural parents and the child, except that stepparent adoptions do not curtail the inheritance rights of the natural parent or the child if the natural parent is married to the stepparent. If an adopted child dies intestate, her property is distributed among those individuals who would have been her heirs had she actually been born to her adoptive parents.

However, under California law, such a child may qualify as a child of the parent if the following requirements are met: ii) Notice must be given to his personal representative of such writing within four months of the issuance of the death certificate; and iii) The child must be conceived and in utero within two years of the issuance of the death certificate.

Calculating Intestate Shares
In California, if surviving issue are all of equal degree of kinship, then the property passes per capita, which simply divides the property equally among the issue. If they are of unequal degree of kinship and the decedent dies intestate or does not specify the method of calculating shares, then the per capita with representation method is used.

rules.rule_statement: Rule The per capita with representation approach divides the property equally among the first generation in which at least one member survives the decedent, with the shares of each member of that generation who does not survive the decedent passing to the then-living issue of the nonliving member. If the nonliving member has no then-living issue, then the nonliving member does not receive a share. Example 1: In the figure above, A dies and is predeceased by her children, B, C, and D, all of whom have surviving issue. Application of a per capita with representation scheme causes A’s estate to be first divided at E’s generation because B’s generation has no surviving members. At E’s generation, the estate is distributed among all living members and all nonliving members who are survived by issue. Because I is deceased without issue, I does not receive a share. E, F, G, and H each take one-fourth, but because E and G predeceased A, their shares must be distributed to their respective surviving issue. At J’s generation, J takes E’s one-fourth share in its entirety, and K and L divide G’s one-fourth share equally.

rules.rule_statement: Per stirpes distribution can be used if the will calls for it. Rule A distribution occurs per stirpes when the issue take in equal portions the share that their deceased ancestor would have taken, if living. The estate is first divided into the total number of the ancestor’s surviving children or issue. Example 2: Applying a per stirpes distribution scheme to the family described in Example 1, A’s estate is divided equally at B’s generation even though there are no survivors. Party J takes B’s one-third share, F takes C’s one-third share, and D’s share is split between H (one-sixth) and the children of G (K and L, who take one-twelfth each).

rules.source_text:
Per Stirpes
Per stirpes distribution can be used if the will calls for it. Rule A distribution occurs per stirpes when the issue take in equal portions the share that their deceased ancestor would have taken, if living. The estate is first divided into the total number of the ancestor’s surviving children or issue. Example 2: Applying a per stirpes distribution scheme to the family described in Example 1, A’s estate is divided equally at B’s generation even though there are no survivors. Party J takes B’s one-third share, F takes C’s one-third share, and D’s share is split between H (one-sixth) and the children of G (K and L, who take one-twelfth each).

rules.rule_statement: Rule The per capita at each generation approach, which is another option a testator may choose, divides the property into as many equal shares as there are members of the nearest generation of issue who survive the decedent and deceased members of that generation with issue who survive the decedent. EXAM NOTE: When analyzing fact patterns using the per capita at each generation approach, begin as per capita with representation but then divide the remaining shares equally among the members of the next generation. Example 3: Applying a per capita at each generation scheme to the family described in Example 1, A’s estate is divided the same as when the per capita with representation method is used, except that the shares of E and G are pooled and divided among the next generation equally, leaving J, K, and L with one?sixth each.

Per Capita At Each Generation
Rule The per capita at each generation approach, which is another option a testator may choose, divides the property into as many equal shares as there are members of the nearest generation of issue who survive the decedent and deceased members of that generation with issue who survive the decedent. EXAM NOTE: When analyzing fact patterns using the per capita at each generation approach, begin as per capita with representation but then divide the remaining shares equally among the members of the next generation. Example 3: Applying a per capita at each generation scheme to the family described in Example 1, A’s estate is divided the same as when the per capita with representation method is used, except that the shares of E and G are pooled and divided among the next generation equally, leaving J, K, and L with one?sixth each.

rules.canonical_name: Portion Attributable To A Predeceased Spouse Or Domestic Partner
rules.rule_statement: If the decedent owns (1) real property that she obtained from a predeceased spouse or domestic partner who died not more than 15 years before her or (2) personal property worth $10,000 or more that she obtained from a predeceased spouse or domestic partner who died not more than five years before her, then that property could go to the heirs of the predeceased spouse or domestic partner. If the decedent has no surviving spouse, domestic partner, or issue, then such property would pass to the issue, parents, or issue of parents of the previously deceased spouse or domestic partner. If these persons do not exist, then such property passes to the decedent’s next of kin; if none, then such property passes to the predeceased spouse’s or domestic partner’s next of kin.

rules.rule_statement: Under common law, the only way for an individual to disinherit an heir was to execute a will disposing of all of his property, because any property not so disposed of could potentially pass to that heir through intestacy. However, even when omitted from a testamentary instrument, certain heirs protected by California law may still be entitled to an intestate share. See § V.A.5. Omitted Spouse or Domestic Partner, infra; see also § V.C. Omitted Children, infra. Cal. Prob. Code § 50. Cal. Fam. Code § 7611(d); Cal. Prob. Code § 6453. Cal. Prob. Code § 6407. Cal. Prob. Code § 6452. Cal. Prob. Code § 6450(b). Cal. Prob. Code § 6451. Cal. Prob. Code § 6454. See Estate of Rivolo, 15 Cal. Rptr. 268 (1961) (Schauer, J., dissenting). Cal. Fam. Code §§ 7600–7730. Cal. Fam. Code § 7630. Cal. Fam. Code §§ 7611, 7573. Cal. Fam. Code § 7611. Cal. Fam. Code § 297.5. Cal. Prob. Code § 249.5. Cal. Prob. Code § 240. Cal. Prob. Code § 6402.5. See, e.g., Estate of Barnes, 407 P.2d 656 (Cal. 1965).

rules.rule_statement: If no surviving spouse, domestic partner, or issue exist, then the property may be distributed to the decedent’s parent(s). If still none, then the property may be distributed to the issue of the parent(s). If still none, then the property may be distributed to grandparent(s) or issue of grandparent(s). If there are no grandparents or issue of grandparents, then the decedent’s estate will pass to any issue of a predeceased spouse or domestic partner, and if none, the estate will pass to the decedent’s next of kin. If the decedent has no surviving next of kin, then the estate will pass to the parents of a predeceased spouse or domestic partner or their issue, and if none, then the estate will escheat to the State of California.

If still none, then the property may be distributed to the issue of the parent(s). If still none, then the property may be distributed to grandparent(s) or issue of grandparent(s). If there are no grandparents or issue of grandparents, then the decedent’s estate will pass to any issue of a predeceased spouse or domestic partner, and if none, the estate will pass to the decedent’s next of kin. If the decedent has no surviving next of kin, then the estate will pass to the parents of a predeceased spouse or domestic partner or their issue, and if none, then the estate will escheat to the State of California.

rules.rule_statement: The parentelic approach follows collateral lines until a live taker is found, at which point the decedent’s property is distributed within that taker’s parentelic line.

The parentelic approach follows collateral lines until a live taker is found, at which point the decedent’s property is distributed within that taker’s parentelic line.

rules.rule_statement: The degree of relationship approach results in those with higher degrees of relationship to the decedent taking to the exclusion of more remote relatives. The degree of relationship is calculated by counting the number of relatives between the living taker and the decedent by using the closest common ancestor.

rules.source_text:
Degree Of Relationship Approach
The degree of relationship approach results in those with higher degrees of relationship to the decedent taking to the exclusion of more remote relatives. The degree of relationship is calculated by counting the number of relatives between the living taker and the decedent by using the closest common ancestor.

rules.rule_statement: The parentelic approach is used as a tiebreaker if the degree of relationship approach results in a tie between multiple living takers sharing the same lowest degree of relationship. Those in the closer collateral line take to the exclusion of those in the more remote collateral line. Cal. Prob. Code § 6402.

rules.rule_statement: A debt owed to the decedent is not charged against the intestate share of any person except the debtor. If the debtor does not survive the decedent, the debt is not considered in computing the intestate share of the debtor’s issue.

rules.rule_statement: By will, a California resident can dispose of all of her separate property and, if she is married or in a domestic partnership, her one-half shares of community property and quasi?community property. Quasi-community property does not apply until death or divorce. Therefore, the decedent has no interest in any property acquired by her spouse that would be quasi-community property until divorce or upon the spouse’s death. For a will to be admissible to probate, the testator must meet the formal execution requirements of the applicable jurisdiction’s Statute of Wills. The testator and witnesses can sign an affidavit under oath before a notary, reciting all of the elements of execution, to demonstrate the validity of the will. In the absence of such proof, the witnesses or the personal representative must attest to the validity of the will following the testator’s death. The validity of a will is the most frequently tested area of wills. Remember that a will requires a writing that the testator signs with present testamentary intent in the joint presence of two witnesses and that both witnesses understand the significance of the testator’s act.

rules.rule_statement: The testator must be at least 18 years of age and of sound mind to create a valid will. In California, a conservator for an individual who lacks testamentary capacity can make a will for that individual if a court orders him to do so. A conservator is a fiduciary and owes the traditional duties of care and loyalty to the conservatee.

rules.rule_statement: California follows the majority view as to what constitutes presence and applies the conscious-presence test. Rule Under the conscious-presence test, both the testator and witnesses must be aware of each other’s presence and of what each is doing, although they are not required to watch each other sign. If the testator does not sign in the witnesses’ presence, she must expressly or impliedly acknowledge her signature to them before they sign.

rules.rule_statement: Witnesses must be of sufficient mental capacity and maturity to comprehend the value of the act of witnessing a will. There is no minimum age requirement. The witnesses need not read the will, but they must be aware that the instrument is a will. Competency is determined as of the time of signing; the subsequent incompetence of a testator or witness does not invalidate the will.

rules.rule_statement: At common law, a witness with a direct pecuniary interest under a will was not competent to witness the will, and such action would result in an invalid will. By statute in most states, including California, an interested witness is competent, and the will is valid. However, unless there were two other disinterested witnesses, a rebuttable presumption arises that the devise to the interested witness is a result of duress, menace, fraud, or undue influence (see § VI.B.3.d. California Statutory Presumption, infra). The presumption does not apply to a gift made to the witness solely in a fiduciary capacity. If the presumption is not rebutted, the witness/devisee may not take more than he would have had the decedent died intestate.

rules.canonical_name: Understand The Nature Of The Act
rules.rule_statement: Rule The testator must execute the will with present testamentary intent. When the testator signs the instrument, he must understand that he is executing a will and must intend that it have testamentary effect. A will is ineffective if the testator intends it only as a joke or intends to accomplish a nontestamentary purpose.

rules.rule_statement: The will consists of all papers that are present at the time of execution and that are intended to form part of the will, established by physical connection of the pages (e.g., stapled together) or by the ongoing consistent nature of the language of the will (e.g., page numbers). Without an execution ceremony, it is difficult to determine what papers were present when a holographic will was executed. However, as long as the testator intends the pages to be part of the will, the fact that they were made at different times will not defeat a finding of integration. EXAM NOTE: The existence of multiple documents might raise the issue of integration, but it could instead raise the issue of incorporation by reference (see § IV.B. Incorporation by Reference, infra).  The rule of integration, as distinguished from incorporation by reference, occurs when there is no reference to a distinctly extraneous document but it is clear that the testator intends two or more separate writings to be her will.  If reading the documents together results in an invalid will, the application of incorporation by reference might save the disposition if the failed document can be incorporated into one that qualiﬁes as a valid will. Cal. Prob. Code § 6101. Cal. Prob. Code § 6110(b)(1)–(2). Cal. Prob. Code § 6110; Estate of Eugene, 128 Cal. Rptr. 2d 622 (Cal. Ct. App. 2002). Cal. Prob. Code § 6100. Cal. Prob. Code § 39. Cal. Prob. Code § 6110(c)(1). Cal. Prob. Code § 6110(c)(1). Estate of Tracy, 182 P.2d 336 (Cal. Ct. App. 1947). Cal. Prob. Code § 6112(a). Cal. Prob. Code § 6112(b)–(d). See Estate of Button, 287 P. 964 (Cal. 1930). Estate of Dumas, 210 P.2d 697 (Cal. 1949).

A holographic will is one in which the material provisions are handwritten by the testator (including property to be distributed and intended beneficiaries) and which is signed by the testator.

rules.rule_statement: The testator need not use a formal signature if her usual signature is not a formal signature (e.g., signing with a nickname or initials). In addition, she need not sign at the end of the document so long as it appears from the document itself that the signature was placed with the intent that it serve as token of execution. A printed will form can be used so long as the material provisions are written by hand.

Common Law
Common law required strict compliance with the formal requirements to create a valid will.

rules.rule_statement: California has adopted a substantial-compliance (harmless-error) approach to determining the validity of a will. A will that was not executed with the required formalities is treated as if it had been executed in compliance if the proponent of the will establishes by clear and convincing evidence that when the testator signed the will, the testator intended the will to constitute the testator’s will. EXAM NOTE: The rule applies to attested and holographic wills as well as codicils.  Begin by fully analyzing the required formalities before discussing this doctrine.  Because a testator’s intent at the time of execution must be established by clear and convincing evidence, absence of a signature cannot be cured by relying on the substantial-compliance (harmless-error) doctrine. Cal. Prob. Code § 6110(c)(2).

rules.rule_statement: The California Probate Code (CPC) references a statutory form that a competent testator may use to create a valid will. For each mandatory clause, the testator must choose an option by signing next to it and filling in the appropriate blanks as instructed. Then, the testator must formally execute the will. Any post-execution additions to or deletions from the will form will be recognized only if shown by clear and convincing evidence to express the testator’s intent. A subsequent dissolution or annulment of the marriage or domestic partnership invalidates any disposition of property, power of appointment, and nomination of former spouse or domestic partner as personal representative, executor, or guardian. Cal. Prob. Code §§ 6220–6221. Cal. Prob. Code § 6226(b). Cal. Prob. Code § 6227.

rules.rule_statement: A conditional will is one in which the gifts in the will are conditioned on the occurrence of some event or the truthfulness of some fact. Extrinsic evidence is allowed to clarify whether the language used constitutes a condition or is only a motive for making the will. Conditional codicils are treated the same as conditional wills, and such conditions are strictly construed in favor of vesting the estate. See Estate of Hilton, 245 Cal. Rptr. 491 (Cal. Ct. App. 1988).

rules.rule_statement: A codicil is a supplement to a will that alters, amends, or modifies the will, rather than replacing it. A codicil must be executed with the same formalities as a will. A holographic codicil to an attested will is valid, as is an attested codicil to a holographic will. A validly executed codicil republishes the will as of the date of the codicil unless the will contains a clause to the contrary. Courts look to the testator’s intent to determine whether to read the provisions of the will as having been republished as of the date of the codicil. To determine whether the document is a new will instead of a codicil, look for a residuary clause, language expressly revoking prior wills, or a disposition of all of the assets. If the will is invalid, a valid codicil may remedy the inadmissibility of the original will, but the codicil will not cure the invalidity. However, the codicil can itself serve as the will and incorporate by reference the defective will as long as the codicil refers to the will with sufficient certainty or is on the same paper as the invalid will (see § IV.B. Incorporation by Reference, infra). Cal. Prob. Code § 88. In re Estate of Baird, 168 P. 561 (Cal. 1917).

rules.rule_statement: A will executed in conformity with California law, with the law of the place where it was executed, or with the law of the state where the testator was domiciled at the time of execution or death is valid in the State of California. A will executed outside of the United States is subject to the Uniform International Wills Act (UIWA). A will is valid irrespective of the place where it was made; the location of the testator’s assets; or the testator’s nationality, domicile, or residence, provided that the will complies with the UIWA. The UIWA requires that the will be in writing, signed, and witnessed by two individuals and a third person who is authorized to act in connection with international wills, who must prepare a certificate to attach to the will. The testator must declare the instrument to be his will and demonstrate knowledge of its contents. Cal. Prob. Code § 6113. Cal. Prob. Code §§ 6381–6384.

rules.rule_statement: When a trustee holds legal title and a beneficiary holds equitable title, there is no need for title to be transferred upon the creator’s death. Thus, inter vivos trust property is nonprobate property. A trust that is silent as to its revocability is deemed revocable.

When a trustee holds legal title and a beneficiary holds equitable title, there is no need for title to be transferred upon the creator’s death. Thus, inter vivos trust property is nonprobate property. A trust that is silent as to its revocability is deemed revocable.

rules.canonical_name: Bank Accounts And Securities Registered In Beneﬁciary Form
rules.rule_statement: Amounts on deposit in a bank account may be transferred at death through a joint account designation or any other multiple-party account designation. The surviving tenant(s) have an absolute right to the account proceeds unless extrinsic evidence is introduced that the decedent added the tenant(s) for convenience only. The owner of a security may register it in beneficiary form so that upon the owner’s death, the security is transferred to the designated beneficiary. Registration in beneficiary form does not limit the rights of a surviving spouse, domestic partner, or creditor.

rules.rule_statement: A decedent may contract with a bank (e.g., Totten trust or payable-on-death bank account), employer (e.g., pension plan), or some other individual or corporation to distribute the property held under the contract at the decedent’s death to a named beneficiary. To collect property held under a payable-on-death contract, the beneficiary must file a death certificate with the custodian holding the property. Many payable-on-death contracts, such as tax-deferred compensation and pension plans, cannot be changed by will because federal law requires the spouse’s consent before the contract can be transferred to someone other than the spouse.

rules.rule_statement: A deed is an effective nonprobate transfer if it is delivered to the grantee before the grantor’s death, even if the grantor retains a life estate in his own favor, as long as the delivery is unconditional. A deed is likewise effective if it is delivered to an escrow agent during the grantor’s lifetime with instructions to turn it over to the grantee upon the grantor’s death. California law authorizes revocable transfer upon death deeds, also called “revocable TOD deeds.” A revocable transfer upon death deed is an instrument that makes a donative transfer of real property to a named beneficiary upon the owner’s death and is revocable until the owner’s death. The deed transfers the real property to a named beneficiary outside the probate process. The person who makes or revokes a revocable transfer upon death deed must have the capacity to contract. To be effective, the deed must be signed by the transferor and two witnesses, dated, acknowledged before a notary public, and recorded, and it must substantially comply with a statutory form.

rules.rule_statement: A joint tenancy exists when two or more individuals own a property with the right of survivorship (i.e., upon the death of a joint tenant, the interest terminates and automatically goes to the surviving joint tenants). Although an interest in a joint tenancy cannot be devised, joint tenants can convey all or part of their individual interests during their lifetime (inter vivos) to a third party, thereby severing the joint tenancy. Code § 5600 et seq. See Cal. Prob. Code §§ 5506, 5042.

rules.rule_statement: A testator can revoke a will (formal or holographic) by executing a later will or codicil (formal or holographic) that partially or completely revokes the prior will. The revocation can be express (e.g., “I hereby revoke all prior wills”) or implied from inconsistent terms in the subsequent instrument. An oral revocation of a will is not valid.

Subsequent Instrument
A testator can revoke a will (formal or holographic) by executing a later will or codicil (formal or holographic) that partially or completely revokes the prior will. The revocation can be express (e.g., “I hereby revoke all prior wills”) or implied from inconsistent terms in the subsequent instrument. An oral revocation of a will is not valid.

rules.rule_statement: If the subsequent instrument does not expressly revoke all prior wills, to the extent possible, the wills (and any codicils) are read together. If there are inconsistencies, the later document controls and revokes the prior will by inconsistency. If a later will has a new or altered residuary clause, it likely revokes the first will by inconsistency. EXAM NOTE: You must be able to distinguish a codicil to an existing will from a new will.  If the subsequent document has a residuary clause, it is likely a new will.  If the subsequent document does not have a residuary clause but the ﬁrst document does, the subsequent document is likely a codicil.

Inconsistency
If the subsequent instrument does not expressly revoke all prior wills, to the extent possible, the wills (and any codicils) are read together. If there are inconsistencies, the later document controls and revokes the prior will by inconsistency. If a later will has a new or altered residuary clause, it likely revokes the first will by inconsistency. EXAM NOTE: You must be able to distinguish a codicil to an existing will from a new will.  If the subsequent document has a residuary clause, it is likely a new will.  If the subsequent document does not have a residuary clause but the ﬁrst document does, the subsequent document is likely a codicil.

rules.canonical_name: Physical Act With Intent To Revoke
rules.rule_statement: A will may be revoked by burning any portion thereof or by canceling, tearing, obliterating, or destroying a material portion of the will with the intent to revoke it. For wills executed in duplicate, all duplicates of the will are revoked if either the testator or another in the testator’s presence and under the testator’s direction destroys one duplicate of the will for the purpose of revoking the will. EXAM NOTE: Both the act and a simultaneous intent to revoke must be proven to effect a valid revocation.  Accidental destruction will not revoke the testamentary instrument.

rules.rule_statement: A testator who lines out text can cancel or reduce a gift but cannot increase a gift by adding new text (interlineation) unless the new text qualifies as a valid codicil. Example: Ann has a valid attested will that gives $5,000 to Pete. She crosses out “$5,000 to Pete,” handwrites “Pete gets $2,000,” and signs in the margin. The gift to Pete of $5,000 is revoked by physical act, and the new gift of $2,000 is valid because it meets the requirements of a holographic will (the sentence “Pete gets $2,000” is a material provision, and Ann signed it). Handwritten alterations or additions to a holographic will made by a testator in whose possession the documents remained at all times will be valid absent a new signature. The prior signature is adopted upon making the new provision if the signature remains intact.

rules.rule_statement: If the testator’s will was last in the testator’s possession, the testator was competent until death, and neither the will nor a duplicate can be found at the testator’s death, then there is a rebuttable presumption of revocation by physical act. The attorney-client privilege does not apply to a lawyer’s testimony concerning the contents of a will if such testimony is relevant to an issue among multiple parties who are claiming through the deceased client. Duplicate originals are two copies of the same will, both validly executed. Destroying one duplicate effectively revokes the other duplicate and all other copies of the will. Destruction of an unexecuted copy will not revoke the original or a duplicate original. Example: A lawyer has a will executed for a client and keeps the original in the office safe. He gives the client a photocopy, which was not signed. The client destroys the copy with an intent to revoke the will. The will remains in effect. A copy of a will, such as a photocopy, cannot be admitted to probate, although it may be used as proof of testamentary intent in the case of a lost or missing will. If a will cannot be found, the burden is on the proponent of the existence of a will to prove the will’s existence by clear and convincing evidence.

rules.canonical_name: Divorce Or Dissolution Of A Domestic Partnership
rules.rule_statement: Divorce or the dissolution of a domestic partnership revokes all will provisions in favor of the former spouse or domestic partner unless the will expressly provides otherwise. Note that if a divorced couple remarries or domestic partners re-register as such before the testator dies, the will provisions concerning the former spouse or domestic partner are revived.

Divorce Or Dissolution Of A Domestic Partnership
Divorce or the dissolution of a domestic partnership revokes all will provisions in favor of the former spouse or domestic partner unless the will expressly provides otherwise. Note that if a divorced couple remarries or domestic partners re-register as such before the testator dies, the will provisions concerning the former spouse or domestic partner are revived.

rules.rule_statement: Rule Revoking a will revokes all codicils thereto, whereas revoking a codicil does not revoke a will but rather revives it. Example: In 2015, a testator executes a will. One year later, he adds a codicil. In 2019, he revokes the codicil with the intention of revoking the will as well. The testator dies in 2025. The 2015 will is offered for probate. It will be admitted because revoking the codicil does not affect the underlying will. Cal. Prob. Code §§ 6120–6124.

rules.rule_statement: Under the common law, revoking a will (or codicil that revoked another will) automatically revived the original will. Most states, including California, no longer support automatic revival of the original will but instead require proof (e.g., by a codicil that republishes the will) that the testator intended to revive the original. Example: Party T executes Will 1 then later executes Will 2 (thereby revoking Will 1 by subsequent instrument). Party T then destroys Will 2 (thereby revoking Will 2 by physical act). The revocation of Will 2 only revives Will 1 if Will 1 still exists and T intended to revive Will 1. Evidence of intention to revive a revoked will is essential to revival of the instrument. Without such evidence, the first will cannot be probated unless it was republished.

Revival By Testator Intent
Under the common law, revoking a will (or codicil that revoked another will) automatically revived the original will. Most states, including California, no longer support automatic revival of the original will but instead require proof (e.g., by a codicil that republishes the will) that the testator intended to revive the original. Example: Party T executes Will 1 then later executes Will 2 (thereby revoking Will 1 by subsequent instrument). Party T then destroys Will 2 (thereby revoking Will 2 by physical act). The revocation of Will 2 only revives Will 1 if Will 1 still exists and T intended to revive Will 1. Evidence of intention to revive a revoked will is essential to revival of the instrument. Without such evidence, the first will cannot be probated unless it was republished.

rules.rule_statement: A testator who revokes a will or portion of a will under a mistaken belief of law or fact—and who would not have revoked the will but for such belief—has not effectively revoked his will. The doctrine of dependent relative revocation (DRR) allows the original will or portion of the will to be revived. Extrinsic evidence is more likely to be admitted to prove a mistake if the revocation was by physical act rather than subsequent instrument, as an instrument is more likely to be interpreted within the four corners of the document. See § IV.F. Ambiguities and Mistakes, infra. EXAM NOTE: If you see an otherwise valid revocation based on a mistake (whether of fact or law), begin your analysis by stating the DRR rule. Example: Party T creates a second will and then writes on the first will, “I am revoking this will because I made a new will.” Party T did not realize that the second will was not valid. The revocation of the first will is set aside, and the first will is given effect. EXAM NOTE: The doctrine should be applied only if doing so honors the testator’s true intent.  Therefore, if the gift is increased in the second will, apply DRR to avoid a forfeiture.  If recognizing the revocation and not reviving the will better reﬂect the testator’s intent (e.g., the gift is decreased in the new will), do not apply DRR. Cal. Prob. Code § 6123.

rules.rule_statement: California uses the term “devise” (noun) to refer to a disposition of real or personal property by will and “devise” (verb) to dispose of such property by will. Classifying assets establishes the order of their distribution and abatement when the estate’s assets are insufficient to satisfy all of the gifts made in the will. Probate courts often classify assets by referencing the testator’s intention when the will was written. Gifts made under the will abate based on how and to whom they are given. Term Example Specific A specific gift is a transfer of specifically “My car to my dentist” identifiable property General A general gift is a transfer to be satisfied from “$100,000 to John” the general assets of the estate Demonstrative A demonstrative gift is a general gift that “$100,000 to John from my X specifies the fund or property from which the account, but if funds are not sufficient, then the rest paid out of transfer is primarily to be made general funds” A residuary gift is a transfer of property that “I give all the rest and residue of my remains after all specific, general, and property, wheresoever situated, whensoever acquired, and whether demonstrative gifts have been satisfied known to me or not, to John” A legacy of shares of stock in a named corporation is usually a specific gift unless surrounding circumstances indicate that the testator intended the bequest to be general. However, unregistered bearer bonds listed on the public stock exchanges are typically general bequests. When there is nothing in the will to show the type of corporation or the manner in which the shares were held, extrinsic evidence is admissible to establish these relevant facts. Cal. Prob. Code § 32. Cal. Prob. Code § 21117(a). Cal. Prob. Code § 21117(b). In re Estate of Painter, 89 P. 98 (Cal. 1907). Cal. Prob. Code § 21117(c). Cal. Prob. Code § 21117(f). In re Estate of Daly, 260 P. 296 (Cal. 1927). Estate of Jones, 141 P.2d 764 (Cal. Ct. App. 1943). Cal. Prob. Code § 6111.5; Estate of Buck, 196 P.2d 769 (Cal. 1948).

rules.rule_statement: California waives the requirement that the document exist when the will was executed if the document referenced in an unrevoked will disposes only of the testator’s personal property and describes the items and recipients with reasonable certainty. The document may not dispose of any single item worth more than $5,000 or items totaling more than $25,000 in value. The writing must be dated and either (1) in the testator’s handwriting or (2) signed by the testator. Cal. Prob. Code § 6130. Cal. Prob. Code § 6132(a). Cal. Prob. Code § 6132(g). Cal. Prob. rules.rule_statement: A will may provide for the designation of a beneficiary or the amount of a disposition by reference to some unattested act or event occurring before or after the execution of the will or before or after the testator’s death if the act or event has some significance apart from the will.

Acts Of Independent Significance
A will may provide for the designation of a beneficiary or the amount of a disposition by reference to some unattested act or event occurring before or after the execution of the will or before or after the testator’s death if the act or event has some significance apart from the will.

rules.rule_statement: If the testator, the beneficiary, or some third person has some control over the act or event, it may still have independent legal significance if it is unlikely that the testator or other person would perform the act solely for testamentary reasons. The execution or revocation of a will of a third person is an act of independent significance. Example: A will might leave a certain gift to “the man who is my niece’s spouse at the time of my death.” The law does not presume that the niece would marry or divorce merely to complete the terms of the will, so her marriage has independent significance.

The execution or revocation of a will of a third person is an act of independent significance. Example: A will might leave a certain gift to “the man who is my niece’s spouse at the time of my death.” The law does not presume that the niece would marry or divorce merely to complete the terms of the will, so her marriage has independent significance.

rules.rule_statement: A pour-over will arises when some or all of the decedent’s probate property is devised to the trustee of an inter vivos trust to be distributed in accordance with the trust. Example: “I give the rest and residue of my estate to the trustee of my inter vivos trust, to hold and distribute pursuant to its terms.” The pour-over clause must be validated by an act of independent significance, through incorporation by reference, or under the Uniform Testamentary Additions to Trusts Act (UTATA). The UTATA, enacted in California, allows property in a decedent’s will to be given to the trustee of an inter vivos trust, even though the trust can be amended or revoked after the will is executed. The trust must be identified in the testator’s will, and its terms must be established in a written instrument (other than a will) executed before or concurrently with the execution of the testator’s will or in the valid last will of a person who has predeceased the testator. Note that beneficiaries of the inter vivos trust are not beneficiaries of the pour-over will because their interest arises under the trust, not the will. Cal. Prob. Code § 6131. Cal. Prob. Code § 6131. Cal. Prob. Code §§ 32, 6300. Cal. Prob. Code § 24.

rules.rule_statement: Under common law, if a beneficiary died before the testator or before the end of a period by which he was required to survive the testator under the will, the gift failed (lapsed) and went to the residue unless the will provided for an alternative disposition. Absent a residuary clause, the gift passed through intestacy. A gift made by will to an individual who was deceased when the will was executed was treated as a lapsed gift.

Lapse
Under common law, if a beneficiary died before the testator or before the end of a period by which he was required to survive the testator under the will, the gift failed (lapsed) and went to the residue unless the will provided for an alternative disposition. Absent a residuary clause, the gift passed through intestacy. A gift made by will to an individual who was deceased when the will was executed was treated as a lapsed gift.

rules.rule_statement: California, as with most states, has adopted an anti-lapse statute. Rule If the gift was made to kindred (blood relation) of the testator (or of his current or former spouse or domestic partner) who predeceased the testator but left issue who survived the testator, the decedent’s issue take the gift. Example: Party T’s will provides, “I give $50,000 to my son, B.” Party B predeceases T. In California, B’s issue would take the $50,000.

Anti-Lapse Statute
California, as with most states, has adopted an anti-lapse statute. Rule If the gift was made to kindred (blood relation) of the testator (or of his current or former spouse or domestic partner) who predeceased the testator but left issue who survived the testator, the decedent’s issue take the gift. Example: Party T’s will provides, “I give $50,000 to my son, B.” Party B predeceases T. In California, B’s issue would take the $50,000.

rules.rule_statement: When a gift is to an entire class and one member of the class dies, the gift will not lapse but will go to the surviving class members. However, if the anti-lapse statute applies because the deceased class member is related to the testator, then the issue of the predeceased member will divide the predeceased member’s share. In California, the anti-lapse statute will not apply to save a gift to a class member who died before the will was executed if the testator knew of this fact. The judiciary will often find the existence of a class gift to avoid a lapsed legacy by noting the group-mindedness of the testator.

Class Gift Rule
When a gift is to an entire class and one member of the class dies, the gift will not lapse but will go to the surviving class members. However, if the anti-lapse statute applies because the deceased class member is related to the testator, then the issue of the predeceased member will divide the predeceased member’s share. In California, the anti-lapse statute will not apply to save a gift to a class member who died before the will was executed if the testator knew of this fact. The judiciary will often find the existence of a class gift to avoid a lapsed legacy by noting the group-mindedness of the testator.

rules.rule_statement: In California, if the residue is left to two or more persons and one dies and if anti-lapse does not apply, then the remaining beneficiaries take in their proportionate shares. This is contrary to the common law, which stated that the testator’s heirs succeeded to any lapsed portion of a residual bequest. In California, if a future interest is left to two or more persons and the gift to one of them lapses, her share passes to the other future interest holders unless anti-lapse applies. Note: A future interest is subject to the Rule Against Perpetuities. California follows the Uniform Statutory Rule Against Perpetuities and takes a wait-and-see approach to determine if the interest will vest within a 90-year perpetuities period before invalidating the interest. Cal. Prob. Code § 21110. Cal. Prob. Code § 21110(c). Cal. Prob. Code §§ 21110(a), 21114. See In re Estate of Steidl, 201 P.2d 58 (1948). Cal. Prob. Code § 21114; See In re Estate of Haney, 344 P.2d 16 (Cal. Ct. App. 1959). Cal. Prob. Code § 21111(b). Cal. Prob. Code § 21205.

rules.rule_statement: The doctrine of ademption applies only to specific bequests. Rule If the subject matter of a specific bequest is missing or destroyed (extinct), the beneficiary takes nothing (not even the insurance proceeds or sales proceeds received for the property by the testator). Courts are inclined to avoid ademption by various means, including the classification of a specific bequest as general or demonstrative, the classification of an inter vivos distribution as a change in form, consideration of the testator’s intent when she disposed of the subject matter, or application of statutory exceptions to the doctrine.

rules.rule_statement: The testator’s intent is not relevant in most states if the bequest is extinct. However, in California, the testator’s intent when she disposed of the subject matter of the bequest is examined. Therefore, ademption does not apply when the testator was incompetent, because an incompetent testator lacks the intent to adeem and the opportunity to avoid the effect of ademption by making a new will, unless the will was executed before the incompetency. If the testator’s guardian sells property specifically bequeathed to named legatees during the testator’s incompetency from which the testator does not recover and the proceeds of the sale can be readily traced and satisfied, the bequest is not adeemed and may be satisfied from the proceeds of the sale.

rules.source_text:
Plain-Meaning Rule
The general rule is that a court will interpret a will using only the four corners of the document and that the admission of extrinsic evidence is prohibited. If the plain meaning is clear, then a court will generally not consider any other evidence.

rules.canonical_name: Unambiguous Will With A Mistake In Expression Of Testator’S Intent
rules.rule_statement: Generally, extrinsic evidence is not admissible to reform an unambiguous will. However, an unambiguous will may be reformed if clear and convincing evidence establishes that the will contains a mistake in the expression of the testator’s intent when the will was drafted and if the evidence establishes the testator’s specific intent at that time. Extrinsic evidence is admissible to establish the mistake and the testator’s actual intent despite the fact that the will itself is unambiguous under the four-corners rule.

rules.rule_statement: Traditionally, there was a distinction between patent and latent ambiguities. Patent ambiguities appeared on the face of the will and were required to be resolved within the four corners of the instrument but without extrinsic evidence, whereas latent ambiguities were not apparent from a reading of the will and could be resolved by extrinsic evidence. California does not distinguish between patent and latent ambiguities, and it allows both to be resolved by extrinsic evidence. If the ambiguity cannot be resolved, then the gift in question becomes part of the residue. Cal. Prob. Code § 21102. In re Estate of Duke, 352 P.3d 863 (Cal. 2015). Cal. Prob. Code § 6111.5. Cal. Prob. Code § 21102. See Cal. Prob. Code § 21102(c). Cal. Prob. Code § 6111.5. Cal. Prob. Code § 21111.

rules.rule_statement: Provisions in a will imposing a forfeit of a gift if the beneficiary should ever marry are invalid as against public policy. rules.rule_statement: Most provisions, however, that might initially be viewed as absolute restraints on marriage have been construed by the judiciary as mere statements of the testator’s motives or attempts by the testator to provide for a person until marriage and, as such, have been upheld. Conditions in partial restraint of marriage are not against public policy if they merely impose reasonable restrictions on marriage or attempt to prevent an ill-advised marriage (e.g., to a specific individual). See Cal. Civ. Code § 710.

rules.rule_statement: In common-law states, the elective share gives the surviving spouse a fraction (often one-third) of the decedent’s estate if the surviving spouse takes the elective share rather than any gift contained in the will. The elective share applies to all property of the decedent, regardless of when it was acquired. The elective share does not exist in California and other community?property states. Instead, the surviving spouse or domestic partner is entitled to a forced share of one-half of the community property and quasi-community property. The spouse or domestic partner must elect to take this share in lieu of all other interests she may have under the testator’s will.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A marriage or a domestic partnership formed after the execution of a will does not invalidate the will but may operate as a partial revocation of the will to provide for the omitted spouse. In such cases, a spouse or domestic partner who is not mentioned in a will is entitled to an intestate share of the testator’s estate unless: ii) The spouse or domestic partner was given property outside of the will in lieu of a disposition in the will; iii) The spouse or domestic partner is party to a valid, voluntary agreement waiving the right to share in the estate (e.g., a premarital agreement); or iv) (1) The spouse was a care custodian of the decedent, who was a dependent adult, and the marriage commenced while the care custodian provided services to the decedent or within 90 days after those services were last provided to the decedent and (2) the decedent died less than six months after the marriage commenced. However, if the spouse who was a care custodian proves by clear and convincing evidence that the marriage between the spouse and the decedent was not the product of fraud or undue influence, the spouse is entitled to an intestate share. Assuming that none of the above is true, the omitted spouse or domestic partner takes an intestate share from the decedent’s estate, though the share taken from the decedent’s separate property cannot exceed one-half the value of the separate property in the estate. To satisfy the omitted spouse or domestic partner’s share, the other gifts in the will may have to abate (see § VIII.C. Abatement, infra). Example: Betty has a will that leaves her entire estate to her mother. Betty meets Ann, and they become registered domestic partners. Betty dies without making a new will. Ann is entitled to an intestate share of Betty’s half of the community property and quasi-community property plus an intestate share of Betty’s separate property not to exceed one-half of the value of the separate property in the estate. Ann retains her half of the community property and quasi-community property. App. 2009); see Estate of Gagnier, 26 Cal. Rptr. 2d 128 (Cal. Ct. App. 1993) (waiver may be valid and enforceable under family or probate law).

rules.rule_statement: At common law, any lifetime gift to a child was presumed to be an advancement of that child’s intestate share. The child had the burden of demonstrating that the lifetime transfer was intended to be an absolute gift that was not to be counted against the child’s share of the estate. If a gift is treated as an advancement, the donee must allow its value to be brought into the hotchpot. The advancement is added back into the estate, and the resulting total estate is divided by the number of children taking. The advancement is then deducted from the total share of the child to whom it was given. Example: Party D died intestate with a probate estate worth $150,000. Party D is survived by children A, B, and C, each of whom had received from D an inter vivos gift ($25,000 to A, $50,000 to B, and $75,000 to C). Under the advancement doctrine, the inter vivos gifts (totaling $150,000) are added back into the estate (for a total of $300,000) and then divided evenly among the three children (so each is entitled to $100,000) minus their individual gifts. As a result, A would take $75,000 (her $100,000 share less the $25,000 already received), B would take $50,000 (his $100,000 share less the $50,000 already received), and C would take $25,000 (his $100,000 share less the $75,000 already received). If a child receives an inter vivos share that exceeds the hotchpot share to which each child is entitled, that child does not take but is not required to pay back into the estate.

The modern trend is to transform this function into a conservatorship, wherein the conservator acts as a trustee for the minor, with annual accounting to the court.

Guardianship
A guardian has minimal power over property and must undergo a difficult process to obtain the necessary court approval to act on a minor’s behalf. The modern trend is to transform this function into a conservatorship, wherein the conservator acts as a trustee for the minor, with annual accounting to the court.

rules.rule_statement: The Uniform Transfers to Minors Act (UTMA), enacted in all states, appoints a custodian to use the property of a minor at its discretion on the minor’s behalf without court approval and with no accounting requirement. The custodian must turn any remaining property over to the minor when the minor attains the legal age of majority as established by state law. In California, an adult is an individual who has attained the age of 18 years. If the beneficiary was a minor when the will was executed but attains legal majority before the testator’s death, the property passes directly to the beneficiary absent an instruction otherwise contained in the will.

rules.rule_statement: The third alternative is to establish a trust for a minor, which is the most flexible of the property arrangements. A testator can tailor a trust specifically to the family circumstances and to his particular desires. Whereas under a guardianship or conservatorship the child must receive the property at age 18, a trust can postpone possession until a time when the donor thinks the child will be competent to manage the property. [See the Themis Trusts outline for further discussion.] Cal. Prob. Code § 6409(a). Cal. Prob. Code § 6409(b). Cal. Prob. Code §§ 3901–3922. Cal. Prob. Code § 3901(a).

rules.rule_statement: A pretermitted child is one who is born or adopted after the execution of a will. If a decedent fails to provide for a pretermitted child, then the omitted, pretermitted child receives a share equal to that which the child would have received had the decedent died intestate. Satisfying the omitted child’s share may cause other gifts in the will to abate (see § VIII.C. Abatement, infra). In California, this protection extends to children whom the testator believed to be deceased or the birth of whom the testator was unaware.

The omitted child statute does not apply (the child will not receive a share of the estate) if: ii) The testator had other children when the will was executed and left substantially all of his estate to the omitted child’s other parent; or iii) The testator provided for the child outside of the will and intended this to be in lieu of a provision in the will. Cal. Prob. Code §§ 21620, 21622; See Estate of Cochems, 247 P.2d 131 (Cal. Ct. App. 1952). Cal. Prob. Code § 21621.

rules.rule_statement: A party cannot take property from a decedent when the party was responsible for the decedent’s death. California law treats the killer as if she had predeceased the decedent.
rules.short_rule_statement: A party cannot take property from a decedent when the party was responsible for the decedent’s death. California law treats the killer as if she had predeceased the decedent.
rules.source_text:
Homicide
A party cannot take property from a decedent when the party was responsible for the decedent’s death. California law treats the killer as if she had predeceased the decedent.

rules.rule_statement: The killing must have been intentional and felonious to bar the killer from taking. For example, involuntary manslaughter and self-defense killings do not fall within the homicide doctrine, although assisted suicides do. Killings that were not intentional and felonious may still cause the court to impose a constructive trust.

rules.source_text:
Intentional And Felonious
The killing must have been intentional and felonious to bar the killer from taking. For example, involuntary manslaughter and self-defense killings do not fall within the homicide doctrine, although assisted suicides do. Killings that were not intentional and felonious may still cause the court to impose a constructive trust.

rules.rule_statement: Jurisdictions are split as to whether the killer’s issue should also be barred from taking. Some jurisdictions treat the killer as if she disclaimed the property, which allows the killer’s issue potentially to take under the anti-lapse, per stirpes, and per capita doctrines. California does not apply the anti-lapse doctrine to save a gift to the killer in favor of the killer’s issue.

California does not apply the anti-lapse doctrine to save a gift to the killer in favor of the killer’s issue.

rules.rule_statement: Because acceptance of a testamentary gift is presumed, a party must actively disclaim if she wishes not to accept it. The disclaiming party is treated as if she had predeceased the decedent, and the property is distributed to the next eligible taker. Disclaimers are most often used for tax purposes or to defeat creditors of the heir or beneficiary.
rules.short_rule_statement: Because acceptance of a testamentary gift is presumed, a party must actively disclaim if she wishes not to accept it. The disclaiming party is treated as if she had predeceased...
rules.source_text:
Disclaimer
Because acceptance of a testamentary gift is presumed, a party must actively disclaim if she wishes not to accept it. The disclaiming party is treated as if she had predeceased the decedent, and the property is distributed to the next eligible taker. Disclaimers are most often used for tax purposes or to defeat creditors of the heir or beneficiary.

rules.rule_statement: The disclaimer must identify the decedent, describe the interest being disclaimed, and state the extent of the disclaimer (partial disclaimers are valid). In California, the disclaimer must be in writing, signed, and filed within a reasonable time after the person able to disclaim acquires knowledge of the interest. A reasonable time is conclusively presumed if the disclaimer was filed within nine months after the decedent’s death or after the interest became indefeasibly vested (whichever occurs later). However, under federal law and for the disclaimer to be valid for tax purposes, the disclaimer must be filed nine months from the later of the decedent’s death or the heir’s or beneficiary’s attainment of 21 years of age.

Requirements
The disclaimer must identify the decedent, describe the interest being disclaimed, and state the extent of the disclaimer (partial disclaimers are valid). In California, the disclaimer must be in writing, signed, and filed within a reasonable time after the person able to disclaim acquires knowledge of the interest. A reasonable time is conclusively presumed if the disclaimer was filed within nine months after the decedent’s death or after the interest became indefeasibly vested (whichever occurs later). However, under federal law and for the disclaimer to be valid for tax purposes, the disclaimer must be filed nine months from the later of the decedent’s death or the heir’s or beneficiary’s attainment of 21 years of age.

rules.rule_statement: A disclaimer can be made by a third party, such as a guardian, custodian, trustee, or personal representative, on behalf of a minor, an incompetent person, or a decedent. A spendthrift clause in a will does not preclude a disclaimer.

Who May Disclaim
A disclaimer can be made by a third party, such as a guardian, custodian, trustee, or personal representative, on behalf of a minor, an incompetent person, or a decedent. A spendthrift clause in a will does not preclude a disclaimer.

rules.rule_statement: Some states, including California, bar an individual from taking if he is guilty of elder abuse, which includes physical abuse, neglect, or fiduciary abuse of a decedent who is a dependent or elder adult. If clear and convincing evidence of the abuse is present, then the abuser is treated as if he had predeceased the decedent. Cal. Prob. Code § 250. Cal. Prob. Code § 250(a). Cal. Prob. Code § 4653. Cal. Prob. Code § 251. Cal. Prob. Code § 252. Cal. Prob. Code §§ 278–279. Cal. Prob. Code §§ 275–277. Cal. Prob. Code § 259.

rules.rule_statement: An insane delusion is a belief for which there is no factual or reasonable basis but to which the testator adheres despite all reason and evidence to the contrary. In California, the courts used the term “insane delusion” before Cal. Prob. Code § 6100.5 was enacted, and an insane delusion does not conclusively mean that the person lacks testamentary capacity. EXAM NOTE: Both undue inﬂuence and fraud require misconduct by a third party, whereas an insane delusion arises independently.
rules.short_rule_statement: An insane delusion is a belief for which there is no factual or reasonable basis but to which the testator adheres despite all reason and evidence to the contrary. In...
rules.source_text:
Insane Delusion
An insane delusion is a belief for which there is no factual or reasonable basis but to which the testator adheres despite all reason and evidence to the contrary. In California, the courts used the term “insane delusion” before Cal. Prob. Code § 6100.5 was enacted, and an insane delusion does not conclusively mean that the person lacks testamentary capacity. EXAM NOTE: Both undue inﬂuence and fraud require misconduct by a third party, whereas an insane delusion arises independently.

rules.rule_statement: The majority rule is that a belief is an insane delusion if a rational person in the testator’s situation could not have reached the same conclusion.
rules.short_rule_statement: The majority rule is that a belief is an insane delusion if a rational person in the testator’s situation could not have reached the same conclusion.
rules.source_text:
Rational Person Test
The majority rule is that a belief is an insane delusion if a rational person in the testator’s situation could not have reached the same conclusion.

rules.rule_statement: Once it is determined that the testator suffered from an insane delusion, it must be shown that this was the sole cause of the testamentary disposition. The majority view, including California, requires but-for causation such that the testator would not have disposed of the property in the same manner but for the insane delusion. EXAM NOTE: Remember to discuss causation when analyzing whether an insane delusion exists.  Unless the insane delusion caused the strange disposition, there is no defect in capacity. Cal. Prob. Code § 6100.5(a). In re Estate of MacCrellish, 141 P. 257 (Cal. 1914). In re Estate of Nigro, 52 Cal. Rptr. 128 (Cal. Ct. App. 1966). See Estate of Nelson, 38 Cal. Rptr. 459, 467 (Cal. Ct. App. 1964) (“It is not every form of insanity, not every mental departure from the normal, which destroys an otherwise valid testamentary act.”). In re Estate of Perkins, 235 P. 45 (Cal. 1925).

rules.rule_statement: The court considers all of the following factors when determining whether a person has exerted undue influence: i) Victim’s vulnerability, including incapacity, illness, disability, injury, age, education, impaired cognitive function, emotional distress, isolation, or dependency, and whether the influencer knew—or should have known—of the alleged victim’s vulnerability; ii) Influencer’s apparent authority, including status as a fiduciary, family member, care provider, health care or legal professional, spiritual adviser, expert, or other qualification; iii) Influencer’s actions or tactics, including: c) Initiating changes in personal or property rights, using haste or secrecy in effecting those changes, effecting changes at inappropriate times and places, and claiming expertise in effecting changes; and iv) Equity of the result, including the economic consequences to the victim, any divergence from the victim’s prior intent or course of conduct or dealing, the relationship of the value conveyed to the value of any services or consideration received, or the appropriateness of the change considering the length and nature of the relationship.

rules.rule_statement: Will contracts include contracts to make a will, contracts to revoke a will, and contracts to die intestate, all of which are controlled by contract law. A testator retains the ability to revoke his will at any time before his death, even if he has executed a valid contract not to revoke the will. In such a case, the revoked will must be denied probate, but the interested parties may bring an action for breach of contract against the decedent’s estate.

rules.rule_statement: A joint will is a will signed by two or more persons that is intended to serve as the will of each. Reciprocal wills are wills with identical or reciprocal provisions. In California, the mutual execution of a joint or mutual will does not create a presumption of a contract not to revoke the will. However, if a contract not to revoke is proved and the second party attempts to make an inter vivos transfer not in accordance with the contract or to revoke her will after accepting the benefits under the first party’s will following the first party’s death, then a constructive trust may be imposed for the original beneficiaries’ benefit. When the first party dies in breach, most courts deny relief because the second party has not been damaged and can change his will to avoid sustaining any damage. There are, however, a few cases when the court treats the contract as an ordinary contract; in such cases, either party can sue for the benefit of his bargain if the other revokes. Generally, if a fact pattern includes a joint will, then a contract not to revoke is an issue being tested. Cal. Prob. Code § 21700(b). See Thompson v. Boyd, 32 Cal. Rptr. 513 (Cal. Ct. App. 1963).

rules.rule_statement: A decedent’s assets as of the date of her death are divided between probate and nonprobate property. Probate property is property that passes under intestacy or under the decedent’s will. Nonprobate property passes under an instrument other than a will (e.g., joint tenancy property, life insurance, and pension plan proceeds). When analyzing how to distribute property, first pass any nonprobate property to those identified in the nonprobate instrument. Any remaining property is probate property, and the takers of probate property depend on whether there is a valid will. If the decedent did not have a valid will (or if there is property not properly disposed of in the will), then the probate property is distributed intestate under the jurisdiction’s statute on distribution. See Cal. Prob. Code § 13050.

rules.canonical_name: Providing For Omitted Spouse And Heirs
rules.rule_statement: To satisfy the share owed to an omitted spouse, domestic partner, or child, the other gifts in the decedent’s will may have to abate. Any share owed is satisfied first from the decedent’s estate not passing by will or trust. If that is not sufficient, then the shares of all beneficiaries taking under the will (or other testamentary instruments) abate pro rata, exempting specific property as necessary to fulfill the obvious intent of the decedent. Cal. Prob. Code § 21401. Cal. Prob. Code § 21402. Cal. Prob. Code §§ 21612, 21623.

rules.rule_statement: Each state has nonclaim statutes that bar creditors from filing claims after a specified time period. Because the decedent’s creditors have the same rights regardless of whether the will is contested, general creditors cannot contest. However, a judgment creditor of a beneficiary under a will may be able to contest if he has a perfected interest that will devolve should the will be set aside.

Decedent’S Creditors
Each state has nonclaim statutes that bar creditors from filing claims after a specified time period. Because the decedent’s creditors have the same rights regardless of whether the will is contested, general creditors cannot contest. However, a judgment creditor of a beneficiary under a will may be able to contest if he has a perfected interest that will devolve should the will be set aside.

rules.rule_statement: A no contest clause (also called an “in terrorem clause”) is an express clause within a will designed to deter a beneficiary from suing over her share by causing her to lose her share entirely if she does so. A no contest clause may also appear in a declaration of trust. California upholds no contest clauses in wills and trusts, but they are subject to strict construction. Filing of creditor claims and actions to resolve questions regarding property are not deemed to be will contests. A no contest clause is only enforced against direct contests to the will or its terms without any probable cause. Direct contests include those challenging the validity of the instrument on such grounds as forgery, lack of execution, lack of capacity, menace, duress, fraud, or undue influence. Cal. Prob. Code § 8270. Cal. Prob. Code § 1043. In re Estate of Harootenian, 238 P.2d 992 (Cal. 1951). San Diego Trust & Sav. Bank v. Heustis, 10 P.2d 158 (Cal. 1932). In re Estate of Philippi, 161 P.2d 1006 (Cal. Ct. App. 1945). Cal. Prob. Code § 21310(c). See Burch v. George, 866 P.2d 92 (Cal. 1994). Cal. Prob. Code § 21312. Cal. Prob. Code § 21311.

rules.rule_statement: A personal representative is either named in the will (executor) or court appointed (administrator) to oversee the winding up of a decedent’s affairs. Each state has a detailed statutory procedure for authorizing an executor or administrator to act on the estate’s behalf. If an executor is not named in a will, the court will appoint an administrator. In California, a nonresident individual may serve as personal representative. An individual cannot serve if he is under the age of majority, incapable of serving, or subject to removal for cause. A non-U.S. resident or business partner of the decedent objected to by an interested person in the estate cannot serve unless named in the decedent’s will. The decedent’s surviving spouse or domestic partner and relatives are prioritized over his creditors when applying for appointment as personal representative. The court may appoint as administrator a person nominated by:

Personal Representative
A personal representative is either named in the will (executor) or court appointed (administrator) to oversee the winding up of a decedent’s affairs. Each state has a detailed statutory procedure for authorizing an executor or administrator to act on the estate’s behalf. If an executor is not named in a will, the court will appoint an administrator. In California, a nonresident individual may serve as personal representative. An individual cannot serve if he is under the age of majority, incapable of serving, or subject to removal for cause. A non-U.S. resident or business partner of the decedent objected to by an interested person in the estate cannot serve unless named in the decedent’s will. The decedent’s surviving spouse or domestic partner and relatives are prioritized over his creditors when applying for appointment as personal representative. The court may appoint as administrator a person nominated by:
